preamble.summary: A lament psalm driven by the anguish of betrayal by a close friend. David cries out under the weight of enemies, expresses a desperate wish to flee like a dove into the wilderness, and then reveals the deepest wound: the betrayer is not a stranger but an intimate companion. The psalm oscillates between terror and trust, ending with a command to cast one's burden on the LORD.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm contains one of the most psychologically raw passages in the Hebrew Bible (vv. 13-15). David's pain is not primarily from enemies — enemies he can handle — but from a friend, a companion (aluf, meyudda), someone with whom he walked to God's house in fellowship. The betrayal of intimacy is more devastating than the hostility of strangers because it corrupts the place where trust lived. The dove-flight fantasy (vv. 7-9) is not escapism but an honest expression of the human wish, under unbearable pressure, to simply disappear. David wants wings — not to fight, not to conquer, but to fly away and rest. The Hebrew Bible rarely grants its heroes the luxury of pretending they are unbreakable.
preamble.friction: The historical setting is uncertain. The description of a friend's betrayal has led to associations with Ahithophel, David's counselor who defected to Absalom's rebellion (2 Samuel 15:12, 31). This identification is plausible but not certain — the psalm's language is general enough to apply to any intimate betrayal. The text of verse 16 (Hebrew) is difficult; the shift between singular and plural enemies, and between second and third person address, suggests either a complex rhetorical strategy or textual disturbance.
preamble.connections: The Ahithophel connection links to 2 Samuel 15-17 and Jesus's betrayal by Judas (the early church read this psalm christologically, as in Acts 1:16-20). The dove imagery connects to Song of Songs 2:14 and Hosea 7:11. The command to 'cast your burden on the LORD' (v. 23) is quoted in 1 Peter 5:7. The description of the friend's smooth speech hiding violence (v. 22) echoes Proverbs 26:23-26.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֥חַ בִּנְגִינֹ֗ת מַשְׂכִּ֥יל לְדָוִֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician on Neginoth, Maschil, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. With stringed instruments.
A maskil of David.
translator_notes: The same superscription as Psalm 54 — stringed instruments accompanying a wisdom psalm. The musical instruction suggests a reflective, measured performance, which contrasts sharply with the emotional turbulence of the psalm's content.

verses.text_hebrew: הַאֲזִ֣ינָה אֱ֭לֹהִים תְּפִלָּתִ֑י וְאַל־תִּ֝תְעַלַּ֗ם מִתְּחִנָּתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Give ear to my prayer, O God; and hide not thyself from my supplication.
verses.rendering:
Give ear to my prayer, God,
and do not hide yourself from my plea.
translator_notes: The verb titallam ('hide yourself') is reflexive — David fears that God is deliberately concealing himself, choosing not to be found. The plea is not just 'hear me' but 'stop hiding from me.' The word techinah ('plea, supplication') suggests an urgent, desperate request — not a routine prayer but a cry from someone with nowhere else to turn.

verses.text_hebrew: הַקְשִׁ֣יבָה לִּ֣י וַעֲנֵ֑נִי אָרִ֖יד בְּשִׂיחִ֣י וְאָהִֽימָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Attend unto me, and hear me: I mourn in my complaint, and make a noise;
verses.expanded_rendering: Three urgent imperatives pile up: ha'azinah ('give ear,' v. 2), haqshivah ('pay attention'), and aneni ('answer me'). David is not calmly petitioning — he is shouting. The reason follows: arid be-sichi ('I am restless/wandering in my anguish'). The verb arid can mean 'to wander, to be unsettled, to toss about' — David's mind is churning, unable to settle. The word siach ('complaint, meditation, anguish') covers the full range from anxious thought to spoken lament. And ahimah ('I groan, I moan') — a guttural sound of distress that transcends words.
translator_notes: The accumulation of prayer-verbs (give ear, pay attention, answer) reveals escalating desperation. Each verb is more demanding than the last. David is not maintaining decorum in prayer — he is a man at the end of his capacity.

verses.text_hebrew: מִקּ֤וֹל אוֹיֵ֗ב מִפְּנֵ֣י עׇקַ֣ת רָשָׁ֑ע כִּי־יָמִ֥יטוּ עָלַ֥י אָ֝֗וֶן וּבְאַ֥ף יִשְׂטְמֽוּנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Because of the voice of the enemy, because of the oppression of the wicked: for they cast iniquity upon me, and in wrath they hate me.
verses.rendering:
Because of the voice of the enemy,
because of the pressure of the wicked —
for they heap trouble upon me
and in anger they bear a grudge against me.
translator_notes: The enemies' weapon is their voice (qol oyev) — words again, as in Psalm 52. The word aqat ('pressure, oppression') suggests squeezing, constriction, being pressed into a tighter and tighter space. yamitu ('they heap, they cause to slide down') — trouble is piled on top of David. And yistemuni ('they bear a grudge') from satam, meaning to harbor lasting hostility — this is not a momentary conflict but sustained, malicious hatred.

verses.text_hebrew: לִ֭בִּי יָחִ֣יל בְּקִרְבִּ֑י וְאֵימ֥וֹת מָ֝֗וֶת נָפְל֥וּ עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: My heart is sore pained within me: and the terrors of death are fallen upon me.
verses.rendering:
My heart writhes within me,
and the terrors of death have fallen upon me.
translator_notes: yachil ('writhes, trembles, convulses') is the verb used for labor pains — David's heart is in the grip of contractions, a pain that comes in waves and cannot be controlled. eimot mavet ('terrors of death') — the dread is not abstract but specific: David genuinely fears for his life.

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְאָ֣ה וָ֭רַעַד יָבֹ֣א בִ֑י וַ֝תְּכַסֵּ֗נִי פַּלָּצֽוּת׃
verses.text_kjv: Fearfulness and trembling are come upon me, and horror hath overwhelmed me.
verses.rendering:
Fear and trembling come over me,
and shuddering covers me.
translator_notes: Three words for terror in sequence: yir'ah ('fear'), ra'ad ('trembling'), and pallatsut ('shuddering, horror'). The escalation is physical: from internal fear to bodily trembling to full-body horror that covers (tekasseni) him like a garment. David is wearing his terror.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֹמַ֗ר מִֽי־יִתֶּן־לִ֣י אֵ֭בֶר כַּיּוֹנָ֑ה אָ֝ע֗וּפָה וְאֶשְׁכֹּֽנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: And I said, Oh that I had wings like a dove! for then would I fly away, and be at rest.
verses.rendering:
And I said, "Who will give me wings like a dove?
I would fly away and find rest.
translator_notes: mi yitten li ('who will give me') is the standard Hebrew idiom for 'if only' — expressing a wish the speaker knows is impossible. David knows he cannot become a dove. The fantasy is therapeutic, not delusional.

verses.text_hebrew: הִ֭נֵּה אַרְחִ֣יק נְדֹ֑ד אָלִ֖ין בַּמִּדְבָּ֣ר סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Lo, then would I wander far off, and remain in the wilderness. Selah.
translator_notes: The midbar ('wilderness') is not merely uninhabited space — in the Hebrew Bible it is the place of encounter with God (Sinai), of testing (Israel's wandering), and of refuge (David's own experience in the Judean wilderness). David wants to return to the wilderness where things were simpler, harder, but honest.

verses.text_hebrew: אָחִ֗ישָׁה מִפְלָ֥ט לִ֑י מֵר֖וּחַ סֹעָ֣ה מִסָּֽעַר׃
verses.text_kjv: I would hasten my escape from the windy storm and tempest.
verses.rendering:
I would hurry to my shelter
from the raging wind and storm."
translator_notes: The storm metaphor (ruach so'ah, sa'ar) captures the chaos of the political and personal crisis. The miflaht ('shelter, escape, refuge') is the place where the storm cannot reach. David closes his dove-fantasy with the image of finding cover from a tempest — the crisis is weather he cannot control, and he needs a roof.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּלַּ֣ע אֲ֭דֹנָי פַּלַּ֣ג לְשׁוֹנָ֑ם כִּֽי־רָאִ֥יתִי חָ֝מָ֗ס וְרִ֣יב בָּעִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: Destroy, O Lord, and divide their tongues: for I have seen violence and strife in the city.
verses.rendering:
Confuse their speech, Lord! Divide their tongues!
For I have seen violence and conflict in the city.
verses.expanded_rendering: David shifts from flight-fantasy to imprecation. balla ('swallow, confuse, destroy') echoes the Babel narrative (Genesis 11:7-9), where God confused (balal) human speech to disrupt a unified project of rebellion. David asks God to do the same to his enemies — fracture their communication, make their conspiracy incoherent. The tongue (lashon) has been the weapon throughout these psalms (52:4, 55:21); now David asks God to turn their weapon against them. The reason: he has seen chamas ve-riv ('violence and conflict') in the city. The crisis is urban, not wilderness — it is happening in the place where community should mean safety.
translator_notes: The Babel allusion is deliberate: just as God disrupted human conspiracy at Babel by dividing language, David asks God to disrupt the conspiracy against him by making his enemies unable to communicate effectively.

verses.text_hebrew: יוֹמָ֣ם וָ֭לַיְלָה יְסוֹבְבֻ֣הָ עַל־חוֹמֹתֶ֑יהָ וְאָ֖וֶן וְעָמָ֣ל בְּקִרְבָּֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Day and night they go about it upon the walls thereof: mischief also and sorrow are in the midst of it.
verses.rendering:
Day and night they circle its walls;
trouble and misery are within it.
translator_notes: The enemies patrol the city walls day and night — whether as besiegers or as corrupt authorities within the city. aven ve-amal ('trouble and misery') are personified as resident forces within the city. The safe space has been corrupted from the inside.

verses.text_hebrew: הַוּ֥וֹת בְּקִרְבָּ֑הּ וְלֹא־יָמִ֥ישׁ מֵ֝רְחֹבָ֗הּ תֹּ֣ךְ וּמִרְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Wickedness is in the midst thereof: deceit and guile depart not from her streets.
verses.rendering:
Destruction is within it;
oppression and deceit never leave its public square.
translator_notes: The rechov ('public square, broad place') was the center of civic life — where commerce, justice, and community gathering took place. When tokh u-mirmah ('oppression and deceit') permanently occupy the public square, the entire civic order has collapsed.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹֽא־אוֹיֵ֣ב יְחָרְפֵ֣נִי וְאֶשָּׂ֑א לֹֽא־מְ֝שַׂנְאִ֗י עָלַ֥י הִגְדִּ֗יל וְאֶסָּתֵ֥ר מִמֶּֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For it was not an enemy that reproached me; then I could have borne it: neither was it he that hated me that did magnify himself against me; then I would have hid myself from him:
verses.rendering:
For it is not an enemy who taunts me —
that I could bear.
It is not a foe who rises against me —
from that one I could hide.
verses.expanded_rendering: Here the psalm reaches its emotional core. David makes a devastating distinction: if an oyev ('enemy') had done this, ve-essa ('I could bear it'). If a mesane'i ('one who hates me') had risen higdil ('magnified himself, made himself great') against him, ve-essater ('I could hide'). Enemies are external, identifiable, and manageable. You know where they stand. You can take cover from them. But what follows — what actually happened — is something from which there is no cover.
translator_notes: The conditional structure ('if it were an enemy... I could bear it') is a rhetorical setup for the devastating revelation in the next verses. David is saying: I am not undone by hostility from the expected direction. I am undone by hostility from where I least expected it.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתָּ֣ה אֱנ֣וֹשׁ כְּעֶרְכִּ֑י אַ֝לּוּפִ֗י וּמְיֻדָּעִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But it was thou, a man mine equal, my guide, and mine acquaintance.
verses.rendering:
But it was you — a man my equal,
my companion, my close friend.
verses.expanded_rendering: The 'you' (ve-attah) lands like a blow. The betrayer is identified not by name but by relationship: enosh ke-erki ('a man like my own rank, my equal'), allufi ('my companion, my close associate' — from elef/aluf, suggesting a chief, a leader, someone of equal standing), and meyudda'i ('my intimate, one who knows me' — from yada, 'to know'). These three terms describe a relationship of mutuality, shared status, and deep knowing. This is not a subordinate who turned — it is a peer, a friend, someone who knew David's inner life. The betrayal destroys not just a relationship but the capacity for trust itself.
translator_notes: If the Ahithophel identification is correct, the description fits precisely. Ahithophel was David's most trusted counselor (2 Samuel 16:23 says his counsel was 'as if one inquired of the word of God'). His defection to Absalom was the most personally devastating event of the entire rebellion.

meyudda'i ('my intimate') from yada — the Hebrew verb for the deepest kind of knowing, used for sexual intimacy, for God's knowledge of Israel, and for the closest human relationships. This person knew David in a way that made the betrayal maximally destructive.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֣ר יַ֭חְדָּו נַמְתִּ֣יק ס֑וֹד בְּבֵ֥ית אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים נְהַלֵּ֥ךְ בְּרָֽגֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: We took sweet counsel together, and walked unto the house of God in company.
verses.rendering:
Together we shared sweet counsel;
we walked in the house of God among the throng.
verses.expanded_rendering: The memory is specific and sacred. yachdav namtiq sod ('together we sweetened counsel') — sod is intimate counsel, the kind shared between close friends in private conversation, and namtiq ('we sweetened') means their shared words were a pleasure, a delight. They enjoyed each other's thinking. Then: be-veit Elohim nehalekh be-ragesh ('in the house of God we walked in the throng') — they worshipped together, side by side, in the crowd of the faithful. The betrayal is not just personal but liturgical: this person stood next to David in worship. They sang the same songs, heard the same words, entered the same sacred space. The corruption of that shared worship is what makes the betrayal so spiritually destructive.
translator_notes: sod ('counsel, secret, intimate circle') is the word used for God's own divine council in Job 15:8 and Psalm 89:7. When David says he shared sod with this friend, he is describing the highest level of human intimacy — the exchange of thoughts that are shared with no one else.

verses.text_hebrew: יַשִּׁימָ֤וֶת עָלֵ֗ימוֹ יֵרְד֣וּ שְׁא֣וֹל חַיִּ֑ים כִּֽי־רָע֖וֹת בִּמְגוּרָ֣ם בְּקִרְבָּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Let death seize upon them, and let them go down quick into hell; for wickedness is in their dwellings, and among them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The imprecation is severe: yashimavet ('let death/desolation come upon them') — a compound that may be read as yashi mavet ('let death seize') or yasshimot ('let desolation come'). Either way, David asks for sudden, unexpected destruction. The phrase yerdu She'ol chayyim ('let them go down to Sheol alive') echoes the fate of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram in Numbers 16:30-33, who were swallowed alive by the earth for rebelling against Moses. David sees his betrayers as rebels against God's anointed — their proper fate is Korah's fate. The reason: ra'ot ('evils') dwell bi-megurim ('in their sojourning/dwelling') and be-qirbam ('within them'). Evil has taken up residence both in their homes and in their hearts.
translator_notes: The Korah allusion (going down to Sheol alive) is the most severe imprecation available in the Hebrew idiom. It invokes the one occasion in the Torah where the earth itself punished rebellion by swallowing the rebels whole. David sees the betrayal of friendship as equivalent to the rebellion against divinely appointed leadership.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲ֭נִי אֶל־אֱלֹהִ֣ים אֶקְרָ֑א וַ֝יהֹוָ֗ה יוֹשִׁיעֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: As for me, I will call upon God; and the LORD shall save me.
verses.rendering:
As for me, I call upon God,
and the LORD will save me.
translator_notes: The emphatic ani ('as for me') sets David apart from the corrupt city and the treacherous friend. Whatever they do, he will call on God. The shift from Elohim to YHWH mirrors Psalm 54:8 — the covenant name appears at the moment of deepest trust.

verses.text_hebrew: עֶ֤רֶב וָבֹ֣קֶר וְ֭צׇהֳרַיִם אָשִׂ֥יחָה וְאֶהֱמֶ֗ה וַיִּשְׁמַ֥ע קוֹלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Evening, and morning, and at noon, will I pray, and cry aloud: and he shall hear my voice.
verses.rendering:
Evening and morning and noon
I cry out in anguish,
and he hears my voice.
translator_notes: The three times — evening, morning, noon — may reflect a practice of praying three times daily (Daniel 6:10). The sequence begins with evening because the Hebrew day begins at sunset. The present tense 'he hears' (vayyishma) expresses confidence: God's hearing is not future hope but present reality.

verses.text_hebrew: פָּ֘דָ֤ה בְשָׁל֣וֹם נַ֭פְשִׁי מִקְּרׇב־לִ֑י כִּֽי־בְ֝רַבִּ֗ים הָי֥וּ עִמָּדִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath delivered my soul in peace from the battle that was against me: for there were many with me.
verses.expanded_rendering: padah ('he redeems') is a verb of ransom and liberation — it describes the freeing of a prisoner or slave by paying a price. God ransoms David's nefesh ('life, soul') be-shalom ('in peace') — not merely absence of conflict but wholeness, completeness, well-being. The phrase mi-qerav li ('from the battle against me') acknowledges that war is being waged. And the reason for confidence: be-rabbim hayu immadi ('for many are with me') — David is not alone, despite the betrayal. Whether this refers to human allies or heavenly forces, the point is that God has not left David isolated.
translator_notes: The verb padah is covenantal — it is used for God's redemption of Israel from Egypt (Deuteronomy 7:8) and for the redemption of the firstborn (Exodus 13:13). David sees his personal rescue as participating in the same pattern of divine redemption.

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׁמַ֤ע ׀ אֵ֗ל וְיַעֲנֵ֗ם וְיֹ֘שֵׁ֤ב קֶ֨דֶם ׀ סֶ֗לָה אֲשֶׁ֤ר אֵ֥ין חֲלִיפ֗וֹת לָ֑מוֹ וְלֹ֖א יָרְא֣וּ אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: God shall hear, and afflict them, even he that abideth of old. Selah. Because they have no changes, therefore they fear not God.
verses.rendering:
God will hear and humble them —
he who sits enthroned from of old.
Selah.
For they have no fear of change;
they do not fear God.
verses.expanded_rendering: God — El, the ancient name of deity — will hear and answer (ya'anem, which can mean 'answer' or 'humble, afflict'). He is yoshev qedem ('enthroned from ancient times, seated from of old'). The enemies' problem is diagnosed: ein chalifot lamo ('there are no changes for them' — they experience no reversal, no consequence, no disruption of their prosperity). Because they have never suffered the consequences of their evil, they have no fear of God. Uninterrupted success has produced the practical atheism of Psalm 53:2 — if nothing goes wrong, why would you believe in a God who judges?
translator_notes: chalifot ('changes, alternations') may refer to changes of fortune, changes of behavior, or the absence of divine discipline. The point is consistent: these people have lived without consequences, and consequence-free existence breeds contempt for God.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁלַ֣ח יָ֭דָיו בִּשְׁלֹמָ֑יו חִלֵּ֥ל בְּרִיתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath put forth his hands against such as be at peace with him: he hath broken his covenant.
verses.rendering:
He stretched out his hands against those at peace with him;
he violated his covenant.
verses.expanded_rendering: The singular returns — this is the betrayer again, the one from verses 13-15. He shalach yadav ('stretched out his hands' — a gesture of violence) against bi-shlomav ('those at peace with him' — people with whom he had shalom, covenant peace). And the deepest charge: chillel berito ('he profaned his covenant'). chillel means to make common, to desecrate, to treat something holy as if it were ordinary. The covenant (berit) — whether a formal agreement, a friendship pact, or the implied covenant of shared worship — has been treated as worthless. The betrayer did not merely break a promise; he desecrated something sacred.
translator_notes: chillel berito ('profaned his covenant') uses the same verb applied to desecrating the Sabbath (Exodus 31:14) or God's name (Leviticus 22:32). The friendship-covenant is treated with the same reverence as religious covenants — violating it is not just rude but sacrilegious.

verses.text_hebrew: חָלְק֗וּ מַחְמָאֹ֥ת פִּיו֮ וּקְרׇב־לִ֫בּ֥וֹ רַכּ֣וּ דְ֭בָרָיו מִשֶּׁ֑מֶן וְ֝הֵ֗מָּה פְתִחֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The words of his mouth were smoother than butter, but war was in his heart: his words were softer than oil, yet were they drawn swords.
verses.rendering:
His speech was smooth as butter,
but war was in his heart.
His words were softer than oil,
yet they were drawn swords.
verses.expanded_rendering: The betrayer's technique is exposed through two devastating comparisons. machma'ot piv ('the buttery-smooth things of his mouth') — his speech was rich, pleasant, easy to swallow. But qerav libbo ('war was his heart') — inside, behind the smooth exterior, he was planning combat. The second comparison intensifies: rakku devarav mi-shemen ('his words were softer than oil') — soothing, healing, the kind of words that make you let down your guard. ve-hemmah petichot ('and they were drawn swords') — the words that felt like medicine were actually blades pulled from their sheaths, ready to cut. The psychology of betrayal is precise: the more soothing the speech, the more dangerous the speaker.
translator_notes: This verse is the classic biblical description of the smooth-talking betrayer. Proverbs 26:23-28 develops the same theme: 'Smooth lips with an evil heart are like glaze on an earthen pot.' The drawn-swords image suggests premeditation — these words were weapons prepared in advance.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשְׁלֵ֤ךְ עַל־יְהֹוָ֨ה ׀ יְהָבְךָ֗ וְה֣וּא יְ֭כַלְכְּלֶךָ לֹא־יִ֘תֵּ֥ן לְעוֹלָ֗ם מ֣וֹט לַצַּדִּֽיק׃
verses.text_kjv: Cast thy burden upon the LORD, and he shall sustain thee: he shall never suffer the righteous to be moved.
verses.rendering:
Cast your burden on the LORD,
and he will sustain you.
He will never allow
the righteous to be shaken.
translator_notes: 1 Peter 5:7 directly echoes this verse: 'Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you.' Peter's Greek (epiripsantes) mirrors the forceful Hebrew hashlek — this is not polite delegation but desperate unloading.

yehavkha is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its meaning is derived from context and cognate languages. The rarity of the word adds to its weight: this is not a standard theological term but a unique expression for the specific thing that weighs on you.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתָּ֤ה אֱלֹהִ֗ים תּוֹרִדֵ֮ם ׀ לִבְאֵ֢ר שַׁ֫חַ֥ת אַנְשֵׁ֤י דָמִ֣ים וּ֭מִרְמָה לֹא־יֶחֱצ֣וּ יְמֵיהֶ֑ם וַ֝אֲנִ֗י אֶבְטַח־בָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: But thou, O God, shalt bring them down into the pit of destruction: bloody and deceitful men shall not live out half their days; but I will trust in thee.
verses.rendering:
But you, God, will bring them down
to the pit of destruction.
Men of blood and deceit
will not live out half their days.
But I — I will trust in you.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse splits the world in two. On one side: God will bring the wicked down to be'er shachat ('the pit of destruction' — the deep cistern, the grave, the place of ruin). The anshei damim u-mirmah ('men of blood and deceit') — those whose hands are bloody and whose words are lies — will not live out even half their natural span. Their violence shortens their own lives. On the other side: va-ani evtach bakh ('but I — I will trust in you'). The emphatic ani ('I') separates David decisively from the fate of the wicked. The final word of the psalm is bakh ('in you') — trust's object. After twenty-four verses of terror, betrayal, fantasy, imprecation, and despair, David's last word is directed at God.
translator_notes: The contrast between 'half their days' and David's trust creates a temporal theology: the wicked's time is cut short; the trusting one's relationship with God has no expiration. The psalm does not promise that the righteous will live long — it promises that trust in God transcends the question of duration.
